(M1000) Inpatient Facilities that patient was discharged within the past 14 days?
NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility [Go to M1021]
Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element):

The patient, Doris Watkins, is an 80-year-old female with a primary diagnosis of unilateral primary osteoarthritis of the left knee (M17.12). She has a significant history of chronic low back pain, carpal
tunnel syndrome, and hypertension, which complicates her overall health status. The patient is experiencing ongoing left knee pain that has been persistent, making her mobility severely limited and

affecting her ability to perform daily activities independently. She has been evaluated for a total knee arthroplasty due to the deterioration of her knee condition, which has been supported by imaging
studies showing lateral meniscus pathology and arthritis.

Given her age, the functional limitations stemming from her knee pain, and the planned surgical intervention, Doris requires skilled nursing care to manage her post-operative recovery effectively. This
includes wound care, medication management, and fall risk monitoring, as she may be vulnerable to complications during her recovery process. Additionally, skilled physical therapy is necessary to
facilitate her rehabilitation post-surgery, aiming to restore mobility and strength in her knee while ensuring her safety.
The medical necessity for home health services is underscored by her recent evaluation indicating the need for surgical intervention, as well as her chronic conditions that necessitate a
comprehensive approach to care. By providing home health services, we can ensure that Doris receives the necessary skilled care in the comfort of her home, which is crucial for her recovery and
overall well-being. The combination of skilled nursing and therapy services is not only warranted but essential for her successful rehabilitation and return to independence.

Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance of
another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or procedure
codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom control for each
condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter the ICD-
10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1 - no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and sequencing
requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be reported in M1023
(Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2: the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control for the
condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W,X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control appropriate for
each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1- Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with difficulty, affecting daily
functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3- Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly controlled; history of re-
hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are separate items and sequencing
Z96.652 - Presence of left artificial knee joint
I12.9 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease W
E11.22 - Type 2 diabetes mellitus W diabetic
K42.9 - Umbilical hernia without obstruction or

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all oral medications reliably and safely, including administration of the correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes injectable and IV medications. (
NOTE: This refers to ability, not compliance or willingness.)
0. Able to independently take the correct oral medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times.
1. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if: (a) individual dosages are prepared in advance by another person; OR (b) another person develops a drug diary or chart.
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person at the appropriate times.
3. Unable to take medication unless administered by another person

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all prescribed injectable medications reliably and safely, including administration of correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes IV medications.
0. Able to independently take the correct medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times
1. Able to take injectable medication(s) at the correct times if: a. individual syringes are prepared in advance by another person; OR b. another person develops a drug diary or chart.
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person based on the frequency of the injection
3. Unable to take injectable medication unless administered by another person

After the patient's first attempt, repeat the words using cues ( "sock, something to wear; blue, a color; bed, a piece of furniture") You may repeat the words up to two more times.

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed):
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated

3. Requires considerable assistance in routine situations. Is not alert and oriented or is unable to shift attention and recall directions more than half the time.
4. Totally dependent due to disturbances such as constant disorientation, coma, persistent vegetative state, or delirium.

Patient's primary source of emotional support:
Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects
Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing
(M1400) When is the patient dyspneic or noticeably Short of Breath?
0. Patient is not short of breath
1. When walking more than 20 feet, climbing stairs
2. With moderate exertion (for example, while dressing, using commode or bedpan, walking distances less than 20 feet)
3. With minimal exertion (for example, while eating, talking, or performing other ADLs) or with agitation
Current ability to feed self meals and snacks safely. Note: This refers only to the process of eating, chewing, and swallowing, not preparing the food to be eaten.

3. Able to take in nutrients orally and receives supplemental nutrients though a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
4. Unable to take in nutrients orally and is fed nutrients through a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
5. Unable to take in nutrients orally or by tube feeding.

(M1306) Does this patient have at least one Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury at Stage 2 or Higher or designated as Unstageable?
(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)
0. No [-> Skip to M1322, Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries at SOC/ROC; Skip to M1324, Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury
(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage:
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact or
open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not obscure
the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often includes
undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable pressure
ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury
(M1322) Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries:

Intact skin with non blanchable redness of a localized area usually over a bony prominence. Darkly pigmented skin may not have a visible blanching; in dark skin tones only it may appear with persistent bl
(M1324) Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable:
(Excludes pressure ulcer/injury that cannot be staged due to a non removable dressing/device, coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar, or deep tissue injury.)
Stage of Most Problematic: Patient has no pressure ulcers or no stageable pressure ulcers

Which of the following signs or symptoms characterize this patient as at risk for hospitalization? (Mark all that apply.)

A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or eating
prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injur
B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room to
room (with or without a device such as cane, crutch or walker) prior to the current illness,
C. Stairs: Code the patient's need for assistance with internal or external stairs (with or without a

D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such as

Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)

Current ability to tend safely to personal hygiene needs (specifically: washing face and hands, hair care, shaving or make up, teeth or denture care, or fingernail care)

Current Ability to Dress Upper Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, pullovers, front-opening shirts and blouses, managing zippers, buttons, and snaps:

PLAN OF CARE ORDERS AND DISCHARGE PLAN
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
The patient is considered homebound because she has significant unilateral primary osteoarthritis of the left knee with imaging-supported meniscal/arthritic pathology and an observable left knee
surgical wound (left knee wound documented 18.8 X 0.1 cm) that produces sharp, activity-provoked pain rated 5/10. The record documents dependence on an ambulation aid (walker) and identifies
the need for assistance with safe mobility and transfers; she is high risk for falls and is unable to tolerate standing or walking for extended periods. Leaving the home requires a considerable and
taxing effort because ambulation provokes severe pain and fatigue, necessitates the use of a walker and human assistance for safe transfers and to navigate vehicle entry/egress or uneven
surfaces, and would require prolonged rest to recover after exertion. The patient also requires skilled wound care (dressing changes performed by clinician with caregiver training noted), medication
management and fall-risk monitoring - interventions that cannot be provided in an outpatient setting while the patient is unable to ambulate safely. Limited allowable absences are restricted to
medically necessary appointments (e.g., preoperative or postoperative visits and therapy) and brief, infrequent outings; routine community activities or transportation are unsafe without
accompaniment and substantial assistance. These documented mobility impairments, wound care needs, pain-limited endurance, and high fall risk objectively support that leaving home is medically

impractical or contraindicated at this time and justify skilled home health services (skilled nursing for wound care/medication and safety monitoring and skilled physical therapy for mobility, gait and
ADMIT PATIENT TO APLUS HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
HH SN Frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week for 2 weeks, and 1 time a week for 2 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication management and reconciliation, pain
assessment, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education.
HH PT Frequency: For evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercise, strengthening, and fall prevention techniques training.
Wound care order: Surgical wound on the left knee: Using aseptic technique, cleansed the left knee with wound cleanser, patted dry with sterile gauze 4x4, applied xeroform dressing,
covered with Silicone dressing. SN to change dressing as needed. CG trained to perform wound care between SN visits. SN to instruct the patient/CG to keep the wound clean and dry.
Patient Personal Goal: I want to have my surgical wound heal well without infection and to manage pain effectively so I can move comfortably and return to my normal activities.
SOC, POC, visit frequencies and current health status of the patient
SOC, POC, visit frequencies and current health status of the patient

SN will continue to assess the patient's overall health condition, patient/PCG understanding of the disease process, functional status, and knowledge of self-care management by the next visit. HH PT for
therapeutic exercise, strengthening, mobility, gait, transfer and fall prevention techniques training.
Patient and caregiver education and training to facilitate timely discharge (POC Element ($484.60 (2.xiii))):
Discharge when goals met. Discharge when wound(s) healed.
Discharge when caregiver willing and able to manage all aspects of patient's care.
Discharge when reliable caregiver available to assist with patient's medical needs.
Discharge when patient is independent in management of medical needs.
Able to return to an independent level of care.
Written notice of discharge provided to patient
To self care when max potential achieved
To outpatient therapy with MD approval
